coredata.dc:title: Experimental analysis of the effect of helical capillary tubes on cooling a cylindrical lithium battery
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Daneh-Dezfuli, Alireza
Razij, Amin
Bahrainian, Seyed Saied
coredata.dc:description: Efficient thermal management is critical for ensuring the safety, reliability, and performance of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs), particularly during high charge/discharge cycles. This study investigates an indirect liquid cooling approach using helical copper tubes (HCT) wrapped around a cylindrical 26,650 LIB. The experimental setup examines the effects of parameters: diameter of the copper tubes (DOC), number of turns (NOT), and flow rate (FLR) on the battery temperature (T-BT). Analysis of the empirical data indicated that the T-BT enhancement was invariant with respect to the specific DOC enlargement factors of 1.26 and 2.5 when the NOT was held constant. At a fixed flow rate, increasing the number of turns consistently reduced the average T–BT for all DOC values, with temperature drops reaching about 4–5 % across pitches, showing similar relative reductions for DOCs of 0.028, 0.036, and 0.07. Conversely, for a fixed DOC, a linear relationship is observed where each supplementary coil turn contribute a mean T-BT’s heat transfer (HTR) improvement of 0.34 %. Upon further investigation, a higher FLR further decreases T-BT but results in a higher pressure drop. Flow rate proved to be the dominant factor in thermal performance, with tube pitch having a secondary effect, as shown by the CCD analysis. At a constant flow rate, increasing the number of turns led to higher pressure drops for all three DOC values, with increases of up to 13.43 % and 16.95 % for DOCs of 0.028 and 0.036, respectively, while the larger DOC of 0.07 exhibited the highest relative increase, reaching 72.73 %.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Helical tube
Li-Ion battery
Heat transfer
Cooling
Pressure drop

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: General schematic of the experimental setup under investigation.
Schematic of the 26,650 LIB and its dimensions.
Experimental setup and its construction steps.
Examination of Experiment Repeatability in the Condition Without Cooling Equipment Installed for the LIB.
General Schematic of the LIB Charging System in a Simple Configuration Without Cooling System Installed.
Measured experimental T-BT in a Simple Configuration Without Cooling System Installed at Different Charging Rates.
Comparison of T-BT Between Shetty et al. [31] and the Present Work in a Simple Configuration Without Cooling System Installed.
Copper Tubes with Different DOCs.
(a) T-BT and (b) Outlet Temperature from the HCT Corresponding to Variations in DOC at 1C, NOT=11, and FLR=200 ml/min.
The Triangular Area Formed Between the LIB and Two Steps of the HCT.
(a) T-BT and (b) Outlet Temperature from the HCT Corresponding to Variations in the NOT of the Copper Tube at 1C, DOT=0.91 mm, and FLR=200 Ml/min.
Total Length of the HCT with Varying NOT and DOC.
(a) T-BT and (b) Outlet Temperature from the HCT Corresponding to Variations in FLR at 1C, DOT=0.91 mm, and NOT=16.
Normal probability plots of residuals and residuals versus run number for pressure drop and battery surface temperature.
2D and 3D contour plots showing the effects of copper pipe pitch (N) and flow rate (m) on battery surface temperature.
2D and 3D contour plots showing the effects of copper pipe pitch (N) and flow rate (m) on pressure drop inside the helical coil.
Desirability values for each parameter and response, shown individually and in combination.
Optimized model showing the effects of parameters on pressure drop and battery surface temperature.
Accuracy and Uncertainty of Equipment.
Pressure Drop Within the Coil Corresponding to Variations in Tube Pitch and DOC at a FLR of 200 mL/min.
Average T-BT Corresponding to Variations in Tube Pitch and DOC at a FLR of 200 mL/min.
Actual and Coded Results from a Central Composite Design for Flow Rate and Copper Pipe Pitch and Their Corresponding Responses.

head.ce:title: Experimental analysis of the effect of helical capillary tubes on cooling a cylindrical lithium battery

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Shahid Chamran University of Ahvaz, Ahvaz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Faculty of Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Shahid Chamran University of Ahvaz, Ahvaz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Efficient thermal management is critical for ensuring the safety, reliability, and performance of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs), particularly during high charge/discharge cycles. This study investigates an indirect liquid cooling approach using helical copper tubes (HCT) wrapped around a cylindrical 26,650 LIB. The experimental setup examines the effects of parameters: diameter of the copper tubes (DOC), number of turns (NOT), and flow rate (FLR) on the battery temperature (T-BT). Analysis of the empirical data indicated that the T-BT enhancement was invariant with respect to the specific DOC enlargement factors of 1.26 and 2.5 when the NOT was held constant. At a fixed flow rate, increasing the number of turns consistently reduced the average T–BT for all DOC values, with temperature drops reaching about 4–5 % across pitches, showing similar relative reductions for DOCs of 0.028, 0.036, and 0.07. Conversely, for a fixed DOC, a linear relationship is observed where each supplementary coil turn contribute a mean T-BT’s heat transfer (HTR) improvement of 0.34 %. Upon further investigation, a higher FLR further decreases T-BT but results in a higher pressure drop. Flow rate proved to be the dominant factor in thermal performance, with tube pitch having a secondary effect, as shown by the CCD analysis. At a constant flow rate, increasing the number of turns led to higher pressure drops for all three DOC values, with increases of up to 13.43 % and 16.95 % for DOCs of 0.028 and 0.036, respectively, while the larger DOC of 0.07 exhibited the highest relative increase, reaching 72.73 %.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: An effective thermal management system (TMS) is essential for maintaining the performance, safety, and durability of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs). The TMS regulates cell temperature during charging, discharging, and normal operation to keep the batteries within their optimal thermal range [1]. Proper temperature control prevents performance degradation, capacity loss, and safety hazards such as thermal runaway, which occurs when rising temperature accelerates internal heat generation and may lead to fire or explosion [2]. Maintaining suitable operating temperatures therefore enhances both safety and long-term stability of LIBs [3]. Cylindrical batteries, widely used in electric vehicles and large-scale energy storage systems, require particularly effective cooling due to their high internal resistance and significant heat generation during high-current operation [4]. These cells, such as those used in Tesla vehicles, can reach critical temperatures without appropriate cooling measures [5]. Their geometry also creates cooling challenges, as heat dissipation is less uniform than in prismatic or pouch cells, especially for centrally located cells within a module [6]. This makes uniform temperature distribution across all cells a key objective of any TMS design [7]. To address these challenges, various cooling strategies are used. Direct liquid cooling systems circulate coolant around or through cells to remove heat efficiently [8], while indirect liquid cooling systems rely on cold plates or heat exchangers in contact with the module surface [9]. In addition, recent research has increasingly explored machine learning, statistical approaches, and artificial intelligence to predict battery behavior and improve thermal management performance [10]. These computational methods are now being applied across different aspects of battery studies, from thermal prediction to system optimization [11], demonstrating their growing importance in LIB research [12]. Sharma and Bora [13] review modern machine-learning techniques used to predict the remaining useful life of lithium-ion batteries. Their study highlights the growing importance of data-driven models for handling nonlinear degradation behavior and demonstrates how ML improves prediction accuracy, supports battery health assessment, and enhances EV reliability. Wang et al. [14] proposed an innovative modular liquid-cooling (LQC) system for cylindrical LIBs used in electric vehicles. Their combined simulation and experimental work examined how coolant flow rate and cooling arrangement (series vs. parallel) influence the thermal behavior of a battery module. They found that increasing the coolant flow rate initially leads to a noticeable reduction in maximum temperature and better uniformity; however, beyond a certain point, additional flow brings minimal improvement. Their results also showed that parallel cooling achieves a more uniform temperature distribution than series cooling. The proposed modular configuration offers flexibility for industrial implementation, making it suitable for large-scale battery systems. Vu et al.[15] introduced a new internal thermal management concept for cylindrical cells that aims to reduce internal thermal resistance by guiding heat away from the cell core. Their design incorporates heat pipes and metal heat spreader disks placed at the top and bottom ends of the cell. Thermal modeling showed a substantial 67.8 % decrease in thermal resistance, significantly reducing temperature gradients inside the battery. Although the design adds some mass and reduces overall energy density, it provides a promising pathway for improving safety and thermal stability in automotive batteries, performing similarly to more complex two-phase cooling techniques at the pack level. Zare et al. [16] explored the use of phase-change materials (PCMs) combined with internal and external fins to enhance the low thermal conductivity of PCMs. Their thermal model, based on the enthalpy-porous method, demonstrated that the integration of fins accelerates heat transfer and reduces internal temperature gradients during both constant and variable heat-generation scenarios. Their proposed system with four fins kept battery temperatures below the optimal limit of 318.15 K and performed better than both natural convection and PCM systems without fins. Zhao et al. [17] developed a hybrid TMS for LIBs that combines direct LQC with forced air cooling. The design includes an external jacket where the coolant is placed between the LIB housing and the jacket to create a direct cooling effect. Optimization of parameters such as the gap between the LIB and jacket, the NOT, the coolant FLR, and fan position showed that the best configuration includes a 5 mm gap, a two-tube structure, and parallel horizontal flow of liquid and air. This system performs well at a discharge rate of 4 and demonstrates the ability to suppress fire in electric vehicles. Li et al. [18] compared side and bottom cooling systems for cylindrical LIB modules. The results showed that side cooling controls temperature better, with a maximum of 30.84 °C, but has less temperature uniformity with a temperature difference of 5.76 °C after one hour of discharge. In contrast, bottom cooling has a lower temperature difference of 2.86 °C. Wang et al. [19] investigated an immersion cooling technique combined with direct cooling tubes. Their results demonstrated that this combined approach greatly extends the battery’s optimal operating time by 150.3 % compared to natural convection and 45.7 % compared to pure immersion cooling. Cooling performance improved with larger spacing between cells, higher coolant flow rates, lower coolant temperatures, and coolants with higher thermal conductivity and lower viscosity. Li et al. [20] further evaluated such indirect LQC systems by measuring cell temperatures at different positions under varying discharge rates and ambient conditions. Their results showed temperature reductions of 1.9 % to 8.1 % depending on the measurement location, confirming the effectiveness of indirect cooling at both positive and negative terminals. Toossi et al. [21] examined a combined direct/indirect cooling strategy for a next-generation 46,800 cell pack. Using CuO nanofluid and HFE-7100, they achieved better temperature uniformity and localized hotspot suppression. Their design reduced maximum temperature by up to 4.9 °C at 3C discharge and improved overall thermal performance by 22.7 %. Increasing inlet flow rate further enhanced cooling efficiency and kept the maximum cell temperature below 40 °C. Gyu et al. [22] proposed another hybrid system integrating indirect liquid cooling with forced air. Numerical optimization identified the ideal operating conditions 10 mm tube diameter, 10 tubes, 0.25 kg/s liquid flow rate, and 0.2 m/s air flow which delivered a maximum temperature of 28.7 °C with a modest temperature difference of 3.2 °C across the module. Li et al. [23] conducted 3D thermal simulations on a LiFePO₄ pack consisting of 18 pouch cells. They analyzed direct and indirect cooling under aggressive discharge rates. At 2C, indirect cooling kept the pack below 40 °C, but at 10C the temperature exceeded 100 °C, indicating inadequate thermal control. Direct air cooling failed at high loads, while fluorinated liquid cooling maintained temperatures around 65 °C and improved temperature uniformity, showing its suitability for high-power applications. Zhou et al. [24] uses 3D simulations to explore how R134a refrigerant and half-helical ducts can directly cool 18,650 cylindrical batteries. By examining changes in duct radius and pitch, the authors propose three improved designs that lower peak temperatures and make heat distribution more uniform, leading to more effective and reliable battery cooling. Dwivedi et al. [25] experimentally evaluate a helical liquid-cooling skeleton for 32,700 cylindrical Li-ion cells, examining discharge rate, coolant inlet temperature, and ambient conditions. The design enhances heat removal and lowers average temperature compared to bare modules, though thermal uniformity remains a key limitation. Bu Sinnah et al. [26] develop an sCO₂-based BTMS for cylindrical Li-ion packs, leveraging near-critical heat-transfer behavior. Their 3D simulations show substantial peak-temperature reductions 16.9 °C (1C), 39.9 °C (2C), and 66.1 °C (3C) with optimal performance at 8 MPa, ∼35 °C, Re = 30,000–40,000, and spacing ratios of 0.25–0.275.

HCTs, as a cooling mechanism, offer a unique advantage due to their geometric design, which can enhance HTR efficiency compared to straight tubes. The HCT shape induces secondary fluid flows, increasing the HTR surface area and improving the convective HTR coefficient. By investigating the impact of altering the NOT and DOCs, engineers can optimize the design to maximize heat dissipation while minimizing fluid dynamic resistance, leading to more efficient cooling systems. Adjusting the NOT and DOCs directly affects the cooling system's performance. A smaller NOT or larger DOC may enhance the cooling effect by allowing more effective fluid contact around the LIB, but this could increase the overall system size and potentially its cost. Conversely, a larger NOT or smaller DOC may make the system more compact but could reduce cooling efficiency. Systematic research, including optimization and sensitivity analysis, allows exploration of these variables to find a balanced solution that provides optimal cooling without excessively increasing the size or cost of the LIB system. Additionally, ensuring compatibility with various operational environments and use cases of 26,650 batteries is essential. Different applications may impose distinct thermal loads on the LIB, requiring a flexible cooling solution that can adapt to various conditions.

Efficient TMS is crucial for the safe and optimal performance of LIBs, especially under high charge and discharge rates. This study examines the effectiveness of LQC using HCTs on a cylindrical 26,650 LIB. Various configurations of the HCTs, differing in DOC and NOT, are evaluated to determine their impact on T-BT regulation. The cooling fluids used in this experimental model include water and radiator coolant. The charging rate is maintained at 2C to minimize potential thermal damage to the LIB. As depicted in Fig. 1 , the focus of this study is on developing a experimental model to investigate the TMS of a 26,650 LIB using a LQC system with HCTs.

According to Fig. 1, the copper tubes are coiled around the cylindrical LIB cells in a HCT configuration. Various configurations of the HCTs, with different sizes and NOTs, are tested. Two types of fluids, water and radiator coolant, are used as the cooling mediums. A pump circulates the coolant through the HCTs. Temperature sensors are strategically placed at critical points to monitor the temperature of the LIB and the coolant. Data collection systems, including a data logger, record temperature data, while pressure sensors log pressure data throughout the experiments. The objective of this study is to provide a comprehensive analysis of the indirect LQC technique using HCTs for the 26,650 LIB. The findings from this research will contribute to the development of more efficient TMSs, enhancing the safety and performance of LIBs in various applications.

The primary reason for selecting helical copper tubes (HCTs) as a cooling mechanism is their superior thermal management performance compared to conventional methods such as air cooling, phase-change materials (PCM), or straight liquid-cooled channels [27]. The helical geometry induces secondary fluid flows, which increase the effective heat transfer surface area and enhance the convective heat transfer coefficient, allowing more efficient heat dissipation from the battery surface while maintaining a compact system size. Compared to air cooling, liquid-cooled HCTs provide faster and more uniform temperature regulation, which is particularly important during high charge/discharge cycle [28,29]. In comparison to straight tubes, the helical design improves fluid mixing and heat transfer without a substantial increase in pressure drop, resulting in optimized thermal performance .Furthermore, the tunable parameters of HCTs, such as the number of turns (NOT) and tube diameter (DOC), make this approach highly adaptable and particularly suitable for cylindrical 26,650 lithium-ion batteries.

The experimental setup evaluates the cooling efficiency of LIBs using a system that includes a 26,650 LIB, as shown in the Fig. 3, integrated into a cooling system. This system consists of a pump, a rotameter, a pressure gauge, and temperature sensors. These components work together to monitor and control the cooling process, ensuring effective TMS of the LIB during operation.

The cooling mechanism is facilitated through a closed-loop system powered by a 12 V DC pump that circulates water. This setup ensures continuous HTR from the LIB to the HCTs. Temperature control and monitoring are crucial for the experiment, achieved using K-type thermocouples directly mounted on the LIB's surface. After installing the tubes and temperature sensors, the entire LIB surface is insulated, allowing precise measurement of temperature variations due to different operational stresses. The data from these sensors, along with voltage and current measurements, are continuously recorded, providing detailed insights into the performance and efficiency of the cooling system. The experimental procedures involve charging and discharging the batteries at various rates, with the discharging process testing LIB performance under load. Additionally, a combined charge and discharge cycle with a five-minute cooling period is included to simulate more realistic usage patterns and test the system's response to TMS under dynamic conditions. This experimental setup aims to evaluate and improve TMS solutions for LIBs through rigorous testing of cooling strategies in a controlled environment. It underscores the importance of efficient cooling systems in maintaining LIB safety and longevity, especially under high-load conditions, making it highly relevant to advancing LIB technology for various applications, from electric vehicles to portable electronic devices.

As we know, no experiment is completely accurate because there are always various sources of error. Therefore, assessing measurement uncertainty in experiments can be useful for better evaluating the results. The primary source of uncertainty mainly originates from the instruments and the experimental environment. Many methods have been developed to analyze the amount of uncertainty in the final result. In uncertainty calculations, all errors are considered simultaneously, which happens rarely. For temperature recording, two devices are used: temperature sensors and a data logger. According to the data from the K-type thermocouples used in this study, the uncertainty is presented in Table 1 . Measurement uncertainty is usually estimated as a range around a measured value, indicating the level of confidence that the true measured value lies within the defined range. The following formula is used to calculate the standard uncertainty of the equipment [30]:
(1)
u = S /
n
(2)
u =
∑
i = 1
n
(
X i
−
X ¯
)
2
n − 1
(3)
X ¯
=
∑
i = 1
n
X i
n

This formula accounts for different sources of uncertainty, including instrumental uncertainty, resolution, and any other relevant factors. In the above equation, u represents the root mean square, S is the standard deviation, n is the number of experimental repetitions, and
X ¯
is the Sample data. According to the manufacturer's data, the voltage measurement unit of the charger has an uncertainty of ±0.5 %, while the current measurement unit has an uncertainty of ±1 %.

The repeatability of experiments, meaning the ability to achieve similar results when experiments are repeated under the same methods and conditions, is crucial for ensuring the accuracy and reliability of experimental data. For instance, the T-BT, shown in Fig. 4, demonstrates that the experimental results are repeatable, thereby confirming the reliability of the data obtained from the instruments. This repeatability ensures the validity of the data and provides a solid foundation for the analysis, prediction, and optimization of LIB performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation and comparison with other research
ce:section.ce:para: The applied current rates were 2 A (0.5C), 4 A (1C), and 6 A (1.5C). The current applied to the LIB was adjusted by the charger, and only a single LIB charging cycle was examined. All charging experiments were repeated three times, with measurements taken on three different days to ensure the repeatability of the data. The ambient temperature and the cell temperature were recorded using a Type K thermometer connected to a Lutron BTM-4208SD data logger. The measurement range of these sensors is generally between −50 °C and +199 °C, with an uncertainty of ±0.1 °C. Based on the experimental setup described above, the T-BT data can be extracted as shown in Fig. 6 .

Heat generation in LIBs during charging heavily depends on the charge rate (C-rate). At a 0.5C charge rate, the charging current is relatively low, resulting in less heat generation. Due to the LIB 's internal resistance, less energy is released as heat, and the T-BT usually rises only slightly above the ambient temperature. This charge rate contributes to a longer LIB lifespan and is suitable for applications that do not require fast charging. In contrast, as the charge rate increases to 1C and 1.5C, heat generation significantly increases. At a 1C charge rate, a higher current flows through the LIB, leading to a more substantial temperature rise. At a 1.5C charge rate, heat generation intensifies dramatically. If adequate cooling systems are not employed as the charge rate increases, the T-BT may reach dangerous levels, potentially damaging the LIB and reducing its lifespan. Therefore, TMS and selecting an appropriate charge rate are crucial for maintaining LIB safety and optimal performance. To validate the data obtained from this experiment, a comparison was made. In this comparison, the T-BT was compared with the work of Shetty et al. [31]. Shetty and colleagues [31] analyzed a lithium-ion cell (LiCoO2 26,650) using the NTGK model and designed a LIB TMS with a novel LQC plate. In Fig. 7 , the T-BT is compared for discharge rates of 1C and 1.5C, and the maximum error for the data is 3.23 %. It is observed that the results from the above research are of good accuracy.
This section presents the results obtained from the experiment. Initially, the graphs of T-BT variations, the outlet temperature from the HCT, and the pressure drop are provided. a. Variations in DOC

Copper tubes are widely used in various industries, particularly in plumbing, heating, and cooling systems, due to their excellent thermal conductivity, corrosion resistance, and malleability. As shown in Fig. 8 , three different DOC s were used for the tubes.

As seen in Fig. 9 , increasing the DOC results in a decrease in the T-BT. In fact, with a larger DOC, a greater surface area of the LIB is exposed to the cooler temperature of the copper tube, leading to enhanced HTR due to the increased HTR surface. The increase in the DOC around the tube increases the contact area between the coil and the LIB, which improves HTR.

Additionally, as the DOC increases, more contact is made with the surface of the tube and the fluid inside it, leading to enhanced HTR from the fluid to the coil and then to the environment. This reduces the thermal resistance between the coil and the LIB and provides a more uniform distribution of heat. Increasing the DOC can result in a more even distribution of heat along the length of the tube, helping to reduce hot spots and make the T-BT more uniform. On the other hand, a larger DOC can facilitate a higher FLR of the cooling fluid, which increases the HTR rate. However, increasing the DOC might also increase hydraulic resistance, necessitating more powerful pumps and leading to higher pressure drops to maintain adequate coolant flow, which will be discussed further. Despite this, the increased HTR can result in a lower T-BT. Conversely, the outlet temperature from the coil increases with the larger DOC, meaning that as the T-BT decreases, the outlet temperature from the coil rises. According to Fig. 10 , the increase in the DOCs enlarges the base of the triangle formed between the LIB and the steps of the HCT. This increased surface area subjected to cooling by the HCT results in a higher outlet temperature and a lower T-BT. b. Variations in the NOT of the Copper Tube

In Fig. 11 , the T-BT and the outlet temperature from the coil are presented for variations in the NOT of the HCT. Since the trend of changes for different DOCs was similar, these temperatures are provided for only one HCT. Changes in the NOT of the HCT have significant effects on surface temperature and HTR. Initially, increasing the NOT improves HTR by increasing the contact surface area. According to the figure, as the NOT of the HCT increases, the contact area between the LIB and the copper HCT also increases. This increased contact area enhances HTR because there is more surface area available for heat exchange. For example, a tube with 21 turns has more contact area compared to a tube with 6 turns, leading to better HTR and a reduction in the surface temperature of the tube. On the other hand, increasing the NOT increases the length of the coolant flow path. With more turns, the coolant has to travel a longer distance. This increased path length allows the coolant to remain in contact with the LIB for a longer period, absorbing more heat in the process. This helps reduce the T-BT. More turns can also contribute to more uniform heat distribution along the LIB, significantly reducing hot spots and increasing temperature uniformity across the LIB surface. However, as will be discussed later, increasing the NOT also increases the hydraulic resistance in the system. This leads to a higher pressure drop, which may require more powerful pumps to maintain the coolant flow. Ultimately, it is observed that increasing the NOT of the HCT improves HTR and reduces the tube's surface temperature.

In Fig. 12 , the size and length of the HCT are presented based on the NOT and DOC. According to this figure, it can be observed that increasing the NOT increases the surface area involved in HTR, leading to a reduction in the T-BT. On the other hand, increasing the DOC also increases the surface area involved in HTR, which results in an increase in the outlet temperature for both an increased NOT and an increased DOC, while the T-BT decreases. c. FLR Variations

As seen in Fig. 13 , with increased LIB discharge time, more heat is generated within the LIB, causing the T-BT to gradually rise. On the other hand, as depicted in Fig. 13, increasing the inlet FLR to the copper tube enhances the HTR rate. In fact, increasing the inlet FLR to the coil reduces the T-BT, as more coolant passes over the surface of the copper coil and absorbs more heat from the LIB. However, in this scenario, the outlet temperature from the coil increases. Essentially, the increased FLR leads to higher velocity, which reduces the hydrodynamic boundary layer, while the thermal boundary layer also decreases, thereby increasing HTR.
Variations in Pressure Drop and Average T-BT

Table 2 presents the measured pressure drop within the coil as a function of tube pitch (NOT) and tube diameter (DOC) at a fixed flow rate of 200 mL/min. For a DOC of 0.028 m, the pressure drop increased from 11.9 bar at N = 6 turns to 13.6 bar at N = 21, corresponding to a relative increase of 13.43 %. For DOC=0.036 m, the pressure drop rose from 9.8 bar to 11.52 bar over the same range of turns, with a maximum relative increase of 16.95 %. For the largest DOC of 0.07 m, although the absolute pressure drop values were lower (1.1 bar at N = 6 to 1.9 bar at N = 21), the relative increase was substantially higher, reaching 72.73 %. These results indicate that the pressure drop is strongly influenced by both DOC and NOT. Smaller DOCs lead to higher pressure drop increments with increasing NOT, whereas larger DOCs show lower absolute values but higher relative changes. Overall, increasing the number of turns consistently raises the pressure drop for all DOCs, and larger DOCs correspond to higher relative variations. This behavior is consistent with fluid dynamics principles, as increasing the flow path length (via higher NOT) or the tube diameter affects flow resistance and, consequently, the pressure drop within the system.

Table 3 presents the average battery temperature (T-BT) as a function of tube diameter (DOC) and number of turns (NOT) at a fixed flow rate of 200 mL/min. For DOC = 0.028 m, increasing the NOT from 6 to 21 reduces the T-BT from 31.28 °C to 29.81 °C, corresponding to a relative decrease of 4.6 %. For DOC = 0.036 m, the T-BT decreases from 30.86 °C to 29.56 °C across the same NOT range, with a relative reduction of 4.2 %. For the largest DOC of 0.07 m, the temperature drops from 29.78 °C at 6 turns to 28.41 °C at 21 turns, a relative decrease of 4.6 %. These results show that both increasing the DOC and the NOT contribute to lower T-BT values. Larger DOC enhances the heat transfer capability of the coil, while a higher number of turns increases the contact surface area, promoting more effective cooling. Although the absolute temperature reduction with increasing DOC is modest compared to the effect of increasing NOT, the combination of a larger DOC and more turns provides the most significant improvement in thermal management. This indicates that for optimal LIB cooling, designs should consider both larger tube diameters and increased coil turns to maintain battery temperature within a safe operational range.

In this study, three parameters copper pipe diameter, pipe pitch, and flow rate were investigated to analyze and optimize the system. It was observed that flow rate, pipe pitch, and pipe diameter had the most significant effects on system performance, respectively. However, due to laboratory limitations, the pipe diameter parameter was excluded from the optimization and modeling process. Therefore, only two parameters, flow rate and pipe pitch, were selected for detailed analysis and optimization using the Central Composite Design (CCD) method. The experiments designed via CCD were conducted, and the collected data were used to fit a second-order polynomial model for flow rate and pipe pitch. The results indicated that flow rate had the most significant impact on the system’s response, followed by pipe pitch. The adequacy, stability, and validity of the fitted model were carefully evaluated, confirming that it accurately captured the interactions between flow rate and pipe pitch. This modeling and optimization process was performed using Design-Expert software, which enabled the identification of the optimal combination of flow rate and pipe pitch to maximize system performance effectively.

Table 4 presents the results of a Central Composite Design (CCD), including two parameters (Factor 1 and Factor 2) and two responses (Response 1 and Response 2). The coded values for the parameters (Factor 1 and Factor 2) are set at levels −1, 0, and 1, representing the low, medium, and high levels of the parameters, respectively. The actual values of the parameters (A and B) correspond to the coded values. For instance, level −1 for parameter A equals 396, and level 1 equals 814. The "Std" column indicates the standard order of experiments, reflecting full factorial, center, and star points. The "Run" column shows the execution order of the experiments. The data in the table can be used to fit a second-order polynomial model, allowing us to evaluate the individual and interactive effects of the parameters on the responses. For example, it is observed that increasing the value of A (from 396 to 814) significantly increases the DP response, while the T-S response shows less variation. This information can be used for system optimization and to determine the optimal parameter conditions.

The results of the Central Composite Design (CCD) analysis for two parameters and two responses are presented. The two parameters are flow rate (A) and pipe pitch (B), and the two responses are pressure drop (DP) and surface temperature (T-S). The analysis includes evaluation of different models (linear, two-factor interaction, quadratic, and cubic), ANOVA (Analysis of Variance), and fitting the final model. The linear model, with a p-value <0.0001, indicates a statistically significant effect and is chosen as the better fit. The analysis shows that the flow rate (A) and pipe pitch (B) have the greatest effects on the system responses (pressure drop and surface temperature), respectively. Quadratic and linear models were selected for accurate prediction of the responses. These findings can aid in optimizing both laboratory and industrial conditions and provide optimal parameter settings for achieving desired system performance, which is further elaborated later.

The normal plot of residuals is shown. This plot is one of the common tools in regression analysis and modeling, used to assess the normality of the residual distribution. Residuals represent the differences between actual values and the model-predicted values. The horizontal axis represents the externally studentized residuals standardized values indicating the distance of each residual from the mean, based on standard deviation. The vertical axis represents the normal % probability, reflecting the ideal normal distribution. The red diagonal line represents the ideal normal distribution. Residual points that lie on or close to this line indicate that the residuals follow a normal distribution, suggesting that the model fits the data well and the normality assumption is satisfied. Points that significantly deviate from the red line may indicate anomalies or outliers, requiring further investigation to identify potential issues in the data.

These normal residual plots for pressure drop and battery surface temperature show in Fig. 14 that most residuals lie close to the normal line, suggesting that the assumption of normality is reasonably valid and the model explains the data well. The plot of externally studentized residuals versus run number is also shown. This type of plot helps evaluate model quality and detect any specific patterns in the residuals. The horizontal axis (Run Number) represents the sequence of experimental runs. The vertical axis shows externally studentized residuals, representing the differences between actual and predicted values. The horizontal line at zero indicates zero residual. Residuals near this line reflect high model accuracy. Red horizontal lines typically represent upper and lower control limits for standardized residuals. Residuals outside these limits may indicate outliers. The plot shows that the residuals are generally close to the zero line and none fall outside the control limits, suggesting good model prediction accuracy and no visible pattern in residuals. Overall, the model appears appropriate and reliable.

Fig. 15 illustrates the influence of two parameters—copper pipe pitch (N) and flow rate (m)—on the battery surface temperature (T-S). These contour plots assist in understanding and optimizing the effect of these parameters on the surface temperature. As the flow rate increases (from left to right on the x-axis), the surface temperature decreases. Conversely, as the copper pipe pitch increases (from bottom to top on the y-axis), the surface temperature increases. These plots clearly indicate that the flow rate (m) has a more significant impact on the surface temperature. Increasing the flow rate leads to a notable reduction in temperature, while increasing the pipe pitch (N) results in only a slight rise in surface temperature. Therefore, for optimal surface temperature reduction, controlling and increasing the flow rate proves to be the most effective strategy.

Fig. 16 demonstrates the impact of copper pipe pitch (N) and flow rate (m) on the pressure drop (DP) inside the copper helical coil. These visualizations facilitate the analysis and optimization of these two parameters' effects on pressure drop. The 2D contour plot shows how pressure drop (DP) varies with changes in flow rate (m) and pipe pitch (N), where the contour lines represent constant values of pressure drop. As the flow rate increases (horizontally from left to right), the pressure drop increases. As the pipe pitch increases (vertically from bottom to top), the pressure drop slightly decreases. The 3D surface plot further reveals that increasing the flow rate (m) generally results in a significant rise in pressure drop (DP), while increasing the pipe pitch (N) causes a modest decrease. The color gradient from blue to red visually represents increasing pressure drop, where blue regions indicate lower DP and red regions indicate higher DP. The results clearly show that flow rate (m) has a greater influence on pressure drop. Thus, for minimizing pressure drop, controlling and reducing the flow rate is the most effective optimization strategy.

Desirability optimization is a statistical and engineering method used to determine the best operating conditions for systems or processes with multiple responses. This method employs desirability functions that transform each response into a value between 0 and 1, where 0 represents the least desirable outcome and 1 the most desirable. For each response, an individual desirability function is defined based on the objective (maximization, minimization, or targeting). The overall desirability is calculated as the geometric mean of individual desirabilities, and the operating conditions that maximize overall desirability are identified as optimal.

The desirability plot in Fig. 17 illustrates how the optimal conditions for the two parameters (A and B) and the two responses (DP and T-S) are determined. The desirability values for each parameter and response are shown both individually and collectively. The desirability for pressure drop is 0.984664, indicating that the optimized conditions are close to the ideal for this response. For surface temperature, the desirability is 0.970003, again indicating a near-optimal condition. The overall desirability is 0.977306, which is the geometric mean of the individual desirabilities, confirming that the combined optimization has been successful. The plot shows that both parameters A and B are optimized, each achieving a desirability of 1, indicating ideal conditions. The desirabilities for DP and T-S, reaching values close to 1 (0.984664 and 0.970003, respectively), confirm that the optimization for these responses has also been effectively achieved. The combined desirability of 0.977306 demonstrates that optimal conditions have been successfully established for all parameters and responses.

Finally, the optimized model presented in Fig. 18 highlights that the flow rate (m) has a more significant effect on both responses pressure drop and surface temperature. Increasing the flow rate (m) results in higher pressure drop (DP) and lower surface temperature (T-S).

This article experimentally investigates the cooling of 26,650 LIBs. In this experiment, the LIB is charged and discharged at a rate of 1C to prevent potential thermal damage. An experimental setup was constructed, and the resulting data were extracted and analyzed. The study examines the effects of the DOC wrapped around the LIB, the NOT (or pitch), and the fluid FLR. Additionally, the T-BT was measured at different discharge rates and compared with previous experimental results. The key findings from this experiment are summarized as follows:

The T-BT significantly decreases with an increase in the DOC. This indicates that a larger DOC establishes more contact with the LIB, enabling better HTR and more effective cooling. Essentially, the larger DOC increases the HTR surface area, leading to a reduction in the T-BT. This highlights the importance of selecting an appropriate DOC in LIB cooling systems.

Increasing the NOTs also results in a reduction in the T-BT. More turns increase the contact surface between the coil and the LIB, enhancing HTR. For example, a coil with 21 turns provides more contact area than a coil with 6 turns, leading to better HTR and a lower T-BT. Therefore, the NOTs plays a crucial role in the efficiency of the cooling system.

Both increasing the DOC and the NOT contribute to a more uniform heat distribution along the LIB. This more even temperature distribution reduces hot spots and improves LIB efficiency. Furthermore, a larger DOC increases the coolant flow, which enhances the HTR rate and reduces the T-BT.

Despite the benefits of increasing DOC and the NOT, technical challenges must also be considered. A larger DOC can increase hydraulic resistance, requiring more powerful pumps to maintain the coolant flow. Additionally, increasing the NOT can lead to a higher pressure drop in the system, necessitating careful design and the use of appropriate equipment.

These findings underscore the importance of optimizing the design of cooling systems for LIBs, particularly in selecting the right DOC and NOT to achieve effective cooling while managing the associated technical challenges.

The analysis confirmed that increasing flow rate significantly reduces battery surface temperature but increases pressure drop, whereas increasing pipe pitch slightly increases temperature and decreases pressure drop, with desirability optimization yielding a highly efficient operational condition.

Alireza Daneh-Dezfuli: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation. Amin Razij: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Seyed Saied Bahrainian: Writing – review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
